Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Welcome to Partnering for Profit. We’re going to continue the conversation today, and on
this call we’ll be focusing on the partnering idea and we’ll be looking at it really from both
sides – from the benefits to the copywriter who wants to partner instead of freelance, and
also with the company that would rather develop a deeper relationship with his copywriter
then he’s able to through freelancing. I hope you’ll be with us for those, I think they’re really
great.
Basically I’ve made the case over and over again, and they say I’m the world’s highest
paid copywriter, and I suspect that may be true. If there’s somebody making more, it’s not
much more. I certainly don’t know who that would be, but it’s not because I’m the world’s
best copywriter.
I think I write pretty good darn copy, and I’d be more than happy to go up against pretty
much anybody out there, but where the extra money comes from, where the money
multiplier is both for myself and for my clients is that I don’t freelance. I develop deeper
relationships with my clients and that allows my clients to grow more quickly, and it allows
me to make three to four times more than the most senior copywriters out there that I
know about.
I want to share these ideas with you guys. There are business owners on this call. I think
if you give partnering a try you’ll never want to go back because you’ll be making more
money and finding the entire process easier and much more pleasant. And also for the
copywriters on the call, the benefits are obvious, not just more money, but more time
actually doing what you do best, which is writing, and less time doing the things that most
copywriters don’t enjoy doing and don’t get paid for, things like lining up clients, selling
yourself, doing research on a new market, or a new product, or a new company every
month. Partnership has just tremendous advantages over freelancing.
It’s great to have this tool in your arsenal and have it available to you, and so that’s what
we’re going to look into a little more deeply today. Let’s go ahead and jump right in. I’ve
tried to lead people through the process of entering into partnerships.
It usually begins, especially if you’re a new copywriter, with selecting the niche that
you’re going to work in with care. You want a niche that you’re going to be writing about
something that either you know about already or look forward to learning about, because
you’re going to be immersed in that subject.
Whether it’s health, or finance, or bodybuilding, or personal security, whatever, you’ve
selected a niche that you’re looking forward to writing about. The niche should also be one
that has a large prospect base and is growing.
As I pointed out on Tuesday, there are some niches that are shrinking in size, like the
direct mail investment newsletter niche. That thing was 10 million prospects just a few

-- 1 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page 2 of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
years ago, and now I’d be surprised if there was more than a million prospects in that
niche today. By prospects I mean people who are currently subscribed to other financial
newsletters. You want a niche that’s growing and that’s substantial to begin with.
The next step is that you’ve selected the best potential partners in that niche. You maybe
went to the library and got the standard rate and data service, the SRDS, and you looked
up the niche that you’re interested in writing for and you’ve identified the companies that
have the largest customer bases.
You’ve researched those companies. You’ve looked at the demographics and
psychographics of their file. You’ve looked at how their customers are buying products,
what kind of offers they’re responding to, whether they’re soft offers, hard offers, continuity
offers, two-step offers, and so forth. You’ve also looked at their hotline names to determine
how quickly those companies are growing.
You’ve identified or isolated a number of those companies that would be just fantastic
if you could work with. You’ve contacted those companies and you’ve established
a relationship with them, and hopefully you’ve done a job or two with them just as a
freelancer, because most of the companies you’re going to be talking to have only worked
with freelancers and that’s the only model they know.
The whole process of moving from a freelance career in which your income is going to be
– obviously, it depends on the quality of the work you do and the response you’re getting,
but as I pointed out on Tuesday, the average salary for staff copywriters is $90,000 a year
according to Yahoo Finance.
The freelance copywriters, the ones that I know, are making six figures. A couple of them
have cracked the $1,000,000 a year mark, but that’s about it. The next level up really is
the partnering level, and there are only a few who are doing it. Most who are doing it are
people I’ve helped get started, people like Parris Lampropoulus and Bob Hutchinson and
Jim Punkre and others.
They’ve established partnerships with companies like Early to Rise, and with Rich
Schefren, and with Soundview Publications down in Atlanta. These guys are making
really good money and they’re on hand to stay above the $1 million mark for a long time.
Partnering is what really got me to the $4,000,000 a year mark, and that’s just with a
couple of clients, so it’s really a great way to go.
But you don’t walk in the door and say, “Hey, I want to be your partner, I want you to give
me seven streams of income, I want you pay me four times more than other copywriters.”
You have to do this in a way that makes sense for the client or it’s not going to work.
The best way really is with a first aid concept and you go in and either it’s a client you’re

-- 2 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
already working with and you enjoy working with them and you’re making money for the
client and you’re earning good royalties, or it’s a new client that you’re just now basically
developing a relationship with. Allow it to be on the freelance basis because that’s what
they’re comfortable with, and go in and prove your value. Create a winner or two.
The next step is you’ve broadened your field of influence within that company. As part of
your research for example on a particular promotion you’ll ask questions about, “How did
you come up with this strategy? How did you come up with this particular offer?
“Have you tested this? Have you tested that? What happens to the new customer after I
bring him in the door with this new copy I’m going to write for you? Is there a gauntlet or a
honeymoon period when he first comes on the file where you handle him differently than
you do your average customer?
“What are the products that you’re going to ultimately sell to this person? How do you sell
to them? Do you do it online, do you do it through the mail, do you do it by phone? How do
you continue to market to the people that I’m bringing in?”
As you have those conversations you’re going to have ideas, and as you put those ideas
on the table – and I recommend that you do it by asking questions like, “Have you tried
this? Have you tested that?” Or saying, “You know, I was doing some research the other
day and I noticed your biggest competitor does it this way. Have you tried that?”
A lot of the ideas that you have as a new writer and a fresh set of eyes on this particular
company and its product are going to be ideas they’ve had before, and some of them are
going to be ideas they’ve tested before and that they think didn’t work, maybe.
Making declarative statements, “Well, if you do this you’ll double your response,” you run
the risk of looking like a bit of a buffoon. It’s always better to just pose it as a question and
lead your client through that way.
At some point during this process, in my experience at least, the client is typically the first
one to raise the issue of a deeper relationship with you. He’s going to want more access to
you. He’s going to want more of your writing time in a year.
He’s going to ask you for an exclusive maybe. He doesn’t want you writing for his
competitors, or he’s going to say something like, “Gee, I wonder if you’d take a look at our
package stuffers,” or “I wish you’d take a look at our customer file marketing.”
When that happens the implication is immediate. If you’re writing for the front end, and
we’ll get more into this in a moment, your compensation is structured a particular way
tailored for the fact that you’re writing for the front end to attract new customers.

-- 3 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
When you begin writing other types of copy, and we’ll look at six other kinds of writing
projects that all of our clients need, the compensation structure that works for new
customer acquisition doesn’t necessarily work.
The minute you begin talking about doing a different kind of copy for the client, that
immediately suggests a different kind of deal with the client and that means it’s time to
close the deal. It’s time to sit down and work out how you’re going to work together moving
forward.
I think one of the key moments in this entire process is immediately after it becomes clear
to the two of you that you want a deeper relationship and that you want to focus more of
your time and attention on that one client. At that moment in time you really need one-on-
one face time with the client.
One of the beauties of this approach of partnering really is that you don’t have to live
where the partner lives, you don’t have to be in the same city or the same state, or even in
the same country. The companies I partner with are all over the place and we get together
face-to-face maybe once a quarter and do all the rest of our work over the phone and by
email. You don’t have to give up your dream of living where you want to live.
But when you’re beginning this new relationship, and certainly when you’re discussing
what kind of agreement you’re going to have going forward, I like to go visit the client. I
want to see his offices. I want to see him face-to-face. I want to look him in the eyes as I
ask questions and as he negotiates with me. And mostly I want to get to know him on a
personal level. You can’t do that in the office.
This is presupposing you’re talking with the owner of the company, which I highly
recommend. If it’s a much larger corporation you’re not going to be, but you’ll be talking
to the chief decision-maker in the marketing area. That’s fine too. But in most of the
companies you’ll deal with it’s the owner who’s going to ultimately make the decision.
I like to take him out to dinner and we’ll sit around, have some sushi, chat, get to know
him. If it’s the beginning of a new relationship, I like to take the Redhead with me and have
him bring his wife with him to dinner and just kind of relax together. During that time it’s a
perfect time to pick his brain in terms of what his objectives are, what his goals are, what
his frustrations are, what he thinks the obstacles to growth are in his company.
You get the benefit of his experience and his intimate knowledge of his own company, his
own market, his own products prior to forming your own impressions after meeting his
staff, which typically will happen the next day.
On one occasion, I went so far as Wendy and I went on a cruise with a new client. You’ll
probably enjoy this. It was really as simple as I came to this guy with an idea for a new

-- 4 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
product and he said, “Okay, who’s going to write the promotion?”
I said, “I will.”
I gave him my standard freelance rates. He says, “Okay, let’s do it.” We did it and we sold
about 40,000 subscriptions in very short order, which was more than he had in his entire
company at the time.
We were talking on the phone one day and he says, “Wow, this is incredible, you’ve more
than doubled the size of my company in just a couple of months.”
I just asked him, “Do you want to take it to the next level?”
And he just said, “Yeah, what do you have in mind?”
I said, “Well, I’ve got some ideas but we really can’t do it in a business environment. It’s
going to require us to sit back, do a little brainstorming, and really talk about it. Why don’t
we take a cruise together?”
So we took a five-day cruise, the four of us together, and it gave us plenty of time away
from the office and interruptions and everything to really establish the kind of working
relationship we wanted going forward. I don’t necessarily recommend it, it’s just something
I did once and it worked out fantastic, and that client has paid me millions since.
Now it’s time to get down to the nut cutting. It’s time to begin thinking about the kind of
relationship you’re going to have going forward. I just put together some rules here. The
goal of both parties here is to create a win-win situation.
The negotiation needs to be friendly, it needs to be relaxed, and you as a copywriter or
you as a business owner need to really listen to and think about what your counterparty is
saying, the meaning behind his words. Is he objecting to a particular clause just because
he’s cheap or is it because he thinks doing that would create maybe an organizational
nightmare in his company?
I’ve had situations like that for example where I said, “I want this percent of these sales
I create.” The client didn’t say, “Well, you know what, that means I’m going to have to
establish this whole new reporting methodology inside the company, and that’s going to
take weeks and my IT department is driving me crazy anyway, and it’s such a pain in the
butt. Isn’t there a better way that we could do this?”
Instead of saying that, he just said, “Eh, I don’t know.” And it was real easy to assume it
was just he was trying to cheap out on me, but the real problem was something that was
solvable, which had to do with reporting and calculating the commission. So really listen as

-- 5 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
you’re going through this and try to understand why certain proposals are being made and
why certain objections are being raised.
Look for opportunities to minimize or eliminate the client’s risk on the various types of
projects you’re going to be doing. If you have an ongoing relationship with the client, if you
know the work is going to be there month after month and/or a retainer or any of the other
things that we’re going to look at in minute, and especially if you own a piece of his back
end, the promotions that are going to his existing customers, you can afford to do things
on the front end that save him money and lower his risk.
You can ask for smaller advances, you can even do things for free as we’ll see in a
moment, so long as that customer base is growing and you’re benefitting from the growth
of the base because you’re receiving a commission on back end sales.
If you’re a copywriter you need to think about why in the world would any business owner
want to do this, and one of the reasons he wants to do it is he loves the idea of you taking
a risk along with him. He loves the idea of how incentivized he thinks you’re going to be if
your guarantee, your front end advance or whatever, is less and you’re living more on a
percentage of what you produce. He’s going to love that.
He’s also going to love the fact that there are some things you’ll do for free. I actually look
for opportunities to do that. This kind of gets into the next point, too. Offer strategic value
or freebies. I look for opportunities to do stuff for free.
The guy is looking for a new CEO and he’s running a classified ad. “Hey, why don’t you
let me at that copy, maybe I can help it a little bit.” Little things like that take me 5 or 10
minutes but mean a lot to the client.
As you’re going through this there’s sometimes a temptation to kind of be too clever by
half in terms of negotiating particular points in a contract. That never does you any good.
Because just as to your client his relationship with a customer is the most important thing,
as a copywriter your relationship with that client is by far the most important thing.
It’s very important that you not get in a situation where you spend the time and money to
negotiate a long-term agreement, a long-term partnership with a client and then in month
one the client realizes that a clause that he agreed to doesn’t really mean what he thought
it was.
It’s very important that the client agree to fulfill certain responsibilities, that in other words
he’s going to provide certain infrastructure, he’s going to review and turn around the
copy within a given period of time, whatever the things are that you need from him. For
example, I require that my clients provide the research required in order for me to write a
project because I may be a good copywriter but I’m not a great researcher.

-- 6 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
It just makes sense, especially in the financial and health areas where the information and
the research is quite technical. It just makes sense to have somebody who’s whole life
is doing that kind of research do the work. So make sure the client’s responsibilities are
clearly delineated and he understands what’s going to be expected of him in order for you
to give him the greatest possible value.
Make sure that in your agreement and in the conversation leading up to the actual
contract that your responsibilities are equally well-stated. Anything that you leave vague
or potentially assumed, either in that discussion or the agreement, is an opportunity for
disappointment down the road or for misunderstanding.
A great agreement also includes carrots and sticks, and we’ll look at a couple of them in
a moment. It includes incentives for you to do your best work, incentives for the client to
provide you what you need in terms of security, prompt payment and so forth, and it also
includes penalties.
The contracts that I’m going to be sending you later today, or certainly by Friday at the
latest -- we’re still working on that PDF because we have to find contracts I want to send
you that show you five different kinds of deals that I’ve done – these are actual contracts,
but we’ve had to go through and expunge any references to the actual clients and even
some of the numbers associated with deal-points because of confidentiality agreements
I’ve had, but the structure of the contracts are there.
You’ll see in there that there’s maybe one clause in all of those contracts that says
that if I rob a bank or do something horribly immoral the contract can be canceled, but
every single one of them has some kind of a provision that the client will pay me on a
certain date or begin paying interest. Make sure that your agreement has rewards and
consequences for lack of performance, and that goes both ways, whether you’re the
business owner or the copywriter.
It’s also important that you discuss and have institutionalized the very foundation for the
compensation for the copywriter, which is a way of measuring and reporting accurately
and in a timely manner the results of the work that’s been done, whether it’s a customer
file promotion or a new customer acquisition promotion.
In some cases from time to time, clients have asked me to sit on their Board Of Directors.
It’s not a good idea. There’s nothing wrong with accepting an invitation to go to a meeting
of the Board, but the directors of a corporation have legal liability.
If there’s any kind of a problem with a regulating authority or any kind of a legal problem
in the company, you could find yourself running up some pretty substantial legal bills. My
advice is just to avoid that, thank them for the compliment and just say, “I’d be more than

-- 7 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
happy to sit in any time I can help.”
Avoid employee status. You’re going to notice in the contracts that we sent you that in
almost every one of them there’s a very clear clause that says that this is work-for-hire,
that you are indeed an independent contractor.
There are a lot of reasons for that, not the least of which is that from the company’s
standpoint if the IRS ever comes in and determines that you are indeed an employee then
the company is going to owe matching FICA contributions on everything they ever paid
you. That’s not a good thing, so there will usually be a paragraph about the fact that your
status is independent contractor.
Become very aware and make sure that certain financial terms are very clearly defined
in the contract. You need to come to an agreement really on the difference between
gross revenues, net revenues, and profit. These terms sound all official and if you go to
accounting school they’ll give you neat little definitions for them, but the fact of the matter
is that every business you go into defines these things somewhat differently.
We’re going to look at them in greater detail in a moment, but it’s very important that the
two of you are on the same page in terms of these definitions.
It’s also very important that both the copywriter and the business owner are aware of the
fact that if the compensation is based on gross revenues, it can be a smaller percentage
than if it’s based on net revenues or on profit. With gross revenues, nothing is deducted.
With net revenues some things are deducted, and on profit everything is deducted
basically.
Obviously, to make the same amount of money when the base metric for your
compensation changes from gross revenues to net revenues, for example, the percentage
commissions that you request should change to reflect that.
More rules of the road: avoid use of the instant violation trap. Not too long ago there was
a guy who wrote a great book on negotiating. I can’t remember the name of it. But he said
his favorite technique was to always negotiate contracts in which the other party was in
violation of a term of the contract at the moment he signed it because that gave him power
in the relationship.
Some people will still use that and you need to look for it. The document that you’re about
to sign that you’re creating as you discuss this relationship you’re going to live or die by it
for a period of time. Should anything happen to the relationship a judge is going to look at
this document and say, “Well, you were in violation of the contract when you signed it,” or
you became in violation at some point.

-- 8 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
So it is very important that you take these clauses in the contract seriously and to make
absolutely sure if there’s anything in there that you’re not in compliance with, especially
at the moment you sign it, that that is expunged or at least qualified. Obviously, once it’s
signed that becomes pretty much your rule for working so you need to stick by it.
If a client asks you for exclusivity, that’s an extra and there should always be a price tag
attached. I will not give up exclusivity. It’s a negotiating point. If a client in a partnership
arrangement doesn’t want me also accepting a partner who’s a direct competitor of his,
there’s going to be a concession I’m going to ask for in return for that.
It’s only fair to do so because when you sign that exclusivity it eliminates income potential
for the copywriter. It’s a negotiation point. Don’t let that just kind of slip in without it being
discussed.
Non-compete agreements I sign cheerfully. The difference here is that an exclusivity
agreement says, “Okay, I won’t work for your competitor.” A non-compete agreement
says, “Look, I’m not going to learn everything I could learn about your company and then
go out and form my own company to compete with you directly.” Signing non-compete
agreements has never been a problem with me because I don’t intend to do that in the first
place.
I’m no lawyer so one of the things that I’m going to say several times on this call is
that a good attorney needs to be a part of this process, regardless of which side of this
negotiation you’re on. I’ve heard from many, many sources, and I’ve never heard anyone
contradict it, that most non-compete agreements are not enforceable anyway because the
courts typically will not deprive a copywriter for example of his right to make a living, even
if this deal fell through.
Non-disclosure clauses, I’m fine with those too. I sign them all the time, but they need to
be as specific as possible. You don’t want to find yourself in a situation where you mention
to somebody that you work for such-and-such a company and that admission itself is
a violation of nondisclosure. Be sure that the contract makes it very clear what kind of
information it is that you are not to disclose.
Typically it would be things like overall marketing strategy. It could be the media or mailing
lists that are working best for the company. Those are the important things, those are the
crown jewels, and they need to be outlined in the nondisclosure as specifically as possible.
Always have an attorney review your agreement before you sign it. It should be obvious,
but it’s awful tempting sometimes to skip that step because it does take a few days and
slows things down, but do it. Get hard copies with live signatures. Don’t just be satisfied
with something that’s faxed to you. Make sure that you get an actual original copy.

-- 9 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page 0 of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Provide copies to your CPA or tax preparer. It’s important that they know how you’re
compensated just so they’ll know, especially at year-end, if there are ways to save you on
your taxes.
We talked about listening carefully to the concerns of both parties. Now some people say
never pay for anything if you’re the copywriter/marketing rainmaker in this equation. What
I like to do is to have my contract have provisions. If I have to come and visit the client, he
is going to pay my airfare and travel expenses and food expenses on the road portal to
portal. If I incur a direct cost while I’m working on one of his projects, he has to reimburse
me, things like that.
But then I turn around and I never bill them for travel, I never bill them for incidental
expenses, and the reason for that is, again, those kinds of things create a sense of loyalty
or inferred obligation. They just help the bonding process with the client. If you’re nickel
and diming them for every phone call.
I don’t know if you’ve ever sat in a lawyer’s office and watched them work, but they have
a little computer and when the phone rings they hit their mouse and a little clock starts
ticking. That client is being charged by the minute for the phone call, and obviously the first
five minutes of every call is, “How you doing? How’s the weather? How’s your wife? How’s
the kids? How about those Mets?”
And then at the end of the call he hits his mouse button again and off to receivables goes
this little memo that says, “Be sure to bill client ABC this much money.” Well, that ticks
people off and there’s no need for it in the kind of relationship you’re trying to build.
Again, any chance you can, bring value for the client or demonstrate your loyalty to the
company. Essentially as a copywriter marketer who’s partnering with a company, your
mission in life now is to make that business owner more successful and richer. Your
job is basically you want him to know at every step of the way that you’d take a bullet
for him. You’re there to make this happen, and this is just another way of doing that, of
demonstrating that.
I suggest that you work through an LLC or a corporation. This gives you some protection
should the company be sued or if there’s other legal problems. It gives you some
protection against any damaged parties coming against your personal assets. Talk to a
lawyer or a good CPA about this if you’re not already working though one of these kinds of
organizations. They’ll give you some good advice on it.
I use an LLC and, I don’t know, my guy says it’s better than a sub chapter S nowadays, so
I’ve gone that way.
This is a big one. When I went to work at Blanchard and Company, the company owner

-- 10 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
said, “I want to pay you on a percentage of the net,” the bottom line. And I said, “What,
are you crazy? That means that you’re going to deduct overhead, which I have no control
over, investment in the company, which I have no control over, your own compensation,
which I have no control over, interest on loans that you take out, which I have no control
over, taxes which I have no control over, amortization.
“I have no control over any of those things. The only thing I have control over is your
response rate and your average unit of sales, so, no, I’m not going to work for net.” So
make sure to avoid allowing these things to sneak into your compensation calculation. We
already discussed this. If costs are deducted prior to your compensation being calculated,
make sure your percentage is adjusted upwards accordingly.
You can’t always do this but it’s nice when you can. Banks still love to play the waiting
game on out-of-state checks, even banks you’ve been with for a long time. My out-of-state
clients, some of them will wire my monthly compensation to me. Others just think it’s too
much of a hassle, but it’s really nice when you can set that up.
This is important, this next one, there’s a personality type – and Wendy’s family is famous
for it – called passive-aggressive. That’s the personality type that sits around and endures
a negative situation and then they go home at night and they kick the dog and they yell
at their spouse. They wouldn’t dream of bringing up the problem with client, but they’ll tell
everyone else who will listen what a pain in the butt the client is.
I’ve often told people what they ought to do is just bring the client home and let the client
kick the dog. It would cut out the middleman. [laughing] But don’t be passive-aggressive.
Be an adult. If there’s a problem, confront it immediately with the client. Just call them
up and say, “Hey, something’s going on here I’m not real comfortable with. Could we talk
about it for a minute?” and just lay it out on the table.
Next month when we do this call, we’re going to be looking at the care and feeding of a
client, of a partner, and some of the problems that you’ll run into when you’re partnering
with a client.
One of those problems is dealing with the staff, and not in all cases are they going to be
thrilled that there’s an outside marketing guy or gal, an outside rainmaker who’s this hero
who’s coming to the company and really making it grow. They’re going to resent you,
and dealing with that resentment is extremely important. Doing it effectively is absolutely
essential.
Protect the relationship at all costs. Remember that the very purpose of an agreement
or a contract is to make sure that everybody is on the same page so that nobody is
disappointed and nobody is surprised. The purpose of this is to protect the relationship.

-- 11 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page 2 of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you’re the business owner, the last thing you want is a marketer who’s making you tens
of millions of dollars to get pissed off and walk because there’s 25,999,000 other small
businesses out there that he can go talk to. The fact of the matter is that they desperately
need his help, too.
If you’re the copywriter and you’ve got a client and the chemistry is right and you’re
making good money and you enjoy the work, the last thing you want to do is to allow the
relationship to deteriorate and lose that client. Protect the relationship at all costs, and
what that means if there’s something that isn’t working – a contract shouldn’t be a “gotcha”
document.
It should be preceded by at least a verbal agreement that, “Hey look, we’re friends, we
know we work well together, we know we’re making money together. We’re doing this to
protect the relationship to make sure that everyone’s on the same page.”
I quite often tell my partners, “Look, I love you, I trust you. There’s absolutely nothing that
I’m worried about in terms of getting paid here. But let’s face it, you could get hit by a bus
tomorrow, somebody else could be running your company and all of the sudden my family
is going hungry because they’re reneging on the agreement that you and I had, so we
have to have this on paper.” And it’s true, those are the reasons why it’s so essential to
have a contract.
I think it’s also important too because in the early going of the relationship when the money
is small it’s not as big a deal. But when your checks get to be $100,000 a month, $200,000
a month, $500,000 a month, some companies and some clients may get a little dodgey
on you. Having a legal document that has recorded their obligation to you in return for the
growth you’ve created really will help you sleep nights.
I want to just move ahead. Some of these thoughts are unrelated but I think they kind of
lead us somewhere, so let’s move on with it.
Keep in mind that there are three kinds of promos that you’re going to work on, and
actually there is a fourth that I thought of later, but there are going to be promotions that
the copywriter is going to work on where there’s no marketing cost and no fulfillment cost.
A typical example of that would be an email campaign selling a product that’s delivered
on the web. Let’s say it’s an email campaign selling a $79 report on investing. There’s no
marketing cost and no fulfillment cost. In this case, gross revenue and net revenue are the
same thing, so your percentage is very easy to calculate.
Then there will be promotions where you have marketing costs but zero fulfillment costs.
This would be like a PPC campaign. I have a client right now that’s spending $350,000
a month on his pay-per-click budget online. But he’s selling a product where he has no

-- 12 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
fulfillment costs. It’s a digital product. In this case, it happens to be an investment advisory
service.
These marketing costs need to be deducted prior to my commission, so my royalty rate is
going to be higher in terms of a percentage on this one.
There are then campaigns where you have both marketing costs and fulfillment costs,
like for example with a health client who’s selling supplements through the mail. Well,
he’s paying $560, $600, or $1,000 to mail the promotion piece, and then on top of that
whenever anybody buys $100 worth of product the client has maybe $20 or $30 worth of
fulfillment costs, product and shipping costs in that.
At each of these levels here you need to be aware that you need to have a different
percentage that you’re going to be working with for the client. What I typically do is I will
use this kind of a campaign kind of as my bellwether, and say, “Well, on this type of a
campaign I want 10% if you’re going to deduct 10% of sales. But on this kind of campaign,
if you’re going to deduct marketing costs first, to make the same amount of money I’m
going to need a little bit higher percentage.”
And same thing here, especially in the early going it’s always good to sit down and do a
spreadsheet and kind of figure out what these costs are and how that percentage changes
as you’re negotiating the deal.
Now when you’re working with your partner, as a freelancer 9 times out of 10 in my career
I was hired to do a new customer acquisition promo, typically a direct-mail promo or web-
based promotions designed to attract new customers from cold lists. That’s the kind of
work most freelancers are doing. It’s not hard and fast. Of course, people are all over the
map with this, but I would hazard a guess that’s probably what most people are doing.
There’s one kind of compensation schedule for that, actually several we’ll look at.
Now the difference between these two is the quantity of these that go out is huge, but the
net revenue or the profit on those kinds of promotion is very small because the response
rate is much smaller because these people don’t know you.
Then there’s customer file promotions. On a customer file your response rate is going to
be higher because they know you, and the average sale is going to be higher because
they trust you. You need to be looking at a different kind of compensation schedule there.
Then there’s house copy, and house copy can be anything – a catalog or a website that
you write once but continues producing sales forever, package stuffers that go out every
time they ship a product that are designed to sell additional product, thank you letters,
renewal series, all of those kinds of things. All of this is copywriting.

-- 13 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Somebody asked on Tuesday, “Do I need to be a marketing consultant or know all about
organizations and procedures and all of that kind of stuff in order to partner?” The answer
is no.
If you’re a copywriter and you have your training like Mark does – Mark Harold wrote
a question. He says, “I’m just starting the AWAI Six Figure Copywriting course. I don’t
have any clients yet. How can I get started in partnering if I don’t have any copywriting
experience?”
I think, Mark, the answer to that is you need to go ahead and finish the course, and you
need to begin as a freelancer and, as we just went over, pick up a few jobs, have a few
successes, and the companies that you have the successes with then become your best
prospects for partnering.
Harun wanted to know who paid for the cruise. [laughing] I don’t even remember. I think he
did, I’m not sure. I think my client did.
As a copywriter, if your only skill is copywriting and you haven’t really gotten into the nuts
and bolts of marketing, but you can still partner doing the first three things on this list.
If however you’ve added to your inventory of tools, and you understand the nuts and
bolts of marketing, and you understand strategy and tactics, you can also get involved in
marketing consultation, which is very nice, where you go in and you give them ideas for
creating new products, or new offers, or new marketing channels or media.
You should be paid for that, and that can be a very lucrative area. Actually, it’s lucrative
because there is no directly measureable number of dollars generated from that, and I’ll
show you why that can be so lucrative in a moment.
Then there’s organizational and procedural consulting. The more you deal with a company
the more you’re going to become aware of situations in the company that are obstacles to
its success. I call them “choke points.”
Maybe it’s organized upside-down. Maybe the marketing people don’t have enough
authority and the financial people have way too much. Maybe the head of the marketing
department needs to be the assistant head of the marketing department and you need to
bring in somebody who’s sharper. Maybe the procedures that they’re using are slowing
things down.
We’ve talked before about the fact that income is measured in time. How much do you
make? I make this much per year. The year has to be there or the amount is meaningless.
Since income, and therefore company growth, is measured in terms of time that means

-- 14 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
that every day that a promotion is delayed this year that means one more promotion is
pushed one day farther into next year at the end of the chain. So anything that slows down
the marketing push is costing that company money, it’s costing that company growth, and
it’s costing you money this year. So turning things around quickly is essential.
A good example of that is in direct mail where you have a number of customer acquisition
promotions per year. Maybe you can get eight of them done per year. But if you could do
nine, you’re going to bring in that many more customers this year and the company will
grow that much faster. On the other hand, if you do seven it has a dramatic affect on the
company’s growth.
Anything to speed that process or to make it more efficient so that the process costs the
client less, he doesn’t have to hire as many people, that kind of thing, has tremendous
value for your client.
Again, simply because there’s no way to really track the dollar amount that you make your
client when you’re doing these things, it presents you with a tremendous opportunity as a
copywriter/marketer/partner.
Finally, there’s deal making. Especially once you’ve been in the market for a while and
you’ve made contacts, you know other companies, you may know vendors – you may be
able to go to your client and say, “Look, I’ve got some time on my hands here. I was just
thinking if we took that campaign we did to our house file last month and if we gave it to
this company here and let him run it to his house file we could probably get half the money
and the names and address of all the people that buy it.”
The marketing is already done, why the heck not, right? If he likes that idea, say, “Well,
look, why don’t I give you a list of 10 companies I’d like to approach with that deal?” You
should be compensated for those kinds of things.
So those are the six kinds of work that you’re going to be doing most often. Within that
there’s seven income streams, seven ways to get paid.
1. 	Royalties
Royalties have to do with paying you per number of eyeballs that see your promotion. In
direct-mail for example, new customer acquisition promotions, direct-mail royalties are
based on the number of pieces mailed. So if you write a promotion you can get say $0.05
for every copy of your promotion that’s delivered through the mail, so it’s based on the
quantity of promotions that are sent.
2. 	Commissions

-- 15 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
This is compensation that’s based on the amount of money you produce. As I pointed out
earlier, if you’re doing customer acquisition, especially through the mail, the cost there is
very high so every dollar you add to that client’s cost means there are fewer pieces he can
mail and break even to generate new customers.
Because he’s not really making any money, he’s trying to break even to create a new
customer, it would be crazy for a copywriter to work for a percentage of profits, right? It
would be crazy for a business owner to pay the copywriter on a basis of a percentage of
revenues, so royalties make sense.
But when you’re promoting to a house file, to an existing customer, commissions make
all the sense in the world, and the copywriter will simply take a percentage of the sales
he produces, or in some cases a larger percentage of the increase in the sales that he
produces each month.
3. 	Overrides
I’ve done deals for example where I come in on the front end and I create new customers,
but Dan Rosenthal is doing the back end marketing. I said, “Hey, look guys. I’m creating all
these customers for my $50/1,000 and I’m making a good living doing that, but I can’t help
but notice that Dan is then going to those people and he is making four times what I’m
making by selling to the back end.
“I know how much Dan is making, so I want a 5% override on those sales on the people
that I produced for you.”
Client says, “Okay, not a problem. Only fair.”
4. 	Retainers
I’ve had quite a few companies over the last 15 years or so put me on retainers. They’re
really nice, especially if you’re doing consulting work. Again, that can’t be tracked in terms
of dollars produced. A retainer is a great way to go -- $10,000, $15,000, $20,000 a month
that is essentially a guarantee against billable hours that you spend giving advice, learning
more about the client’s company, creating better procedures, making recommendations on
organization, and so forth.
I wouldn’t recommend it necessarily have to do with product development and things like
that, because ultimately if you’re doing product development consulting you’re going to get
paid because you’re going to end up doing the promotions.
5. 	Hourly fees

-- 16 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Even without a retainer you can just say, “Look, I’ll do this consulting project with you but I
want $250 an hour,” or whatever your rate is.
6. 	Bonuses based on metrics
Another way is to have bonuses based on metrics where you just say, “If I can lower your
marketing costs this much or if I can lower your lead costs this much, or if I can add this
many new people to your file in this period of time, would you be willing to pay a bonus?”
7. 	Equity interest
On the equity interest side, essentially the way it’s most commonly done is with stock
options. You say, “Okay, it’s a $100-million company. I’m going to be paid directly for the
new customer acquisition stuff I do. I’m going to be paid directly for the stuff I do selling
stuff to your house file, your existing customers.
“But there’s a lot of other things that I’m doing for you, and if you want me to continue
doing them I’m going to need some kind of compensation. So what I’d like to do is a stock
option. You’ve got a $10-million company and you’ve got a million shares of stock out
there valued at $100 a share at today’s level.
“What I want is an agreement that three years from now that I could buy your stock and
instantly resell it to you for $100 a share. That way if I double or triple the stock’s value,
the value of the company during that period of time, when I do that I’ll make all the
compensation I really need.”
Business owners don’t really mind it because you’re not a voting stockholder, you don’t
own stock, you just have an option to buy stock with an agreement that you’ll immediately
sell it back, I mean at the same meeting.
Copywriters like it because you don’t have to actually buy this stock either. All you have to
do is execute the agreement and it all just happens on paper. If you want to you can hand
them a check for $100,000, they’ll hand you a check for $400,000 and you’ll both be on
your way. It offsets. So it’s a really good way to go. It’s made me some great money over
the years.
Also, business owners love it because they get three years of services in the consulting
area without having to really cut you a check for that time, but they get the benefit of
growth that you provide for them. One of the contracts that I’m sending you has that.
Keep in mind that there are some times to work for free and to be really happy about it. I
mentioned it a little bit earlier, but work for free to create an unspoken sense of obligation.
It’s just another way to bond and tighten the relationship that the two of you have together.

-- 17 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
If you’re a company owner and you’re working with a copywriter-marketer and he does
something really good for you, you do something really good for him. Just some little
cheap thing, but acknowledge the fact that, “Wow, this guy is a fantastic team member.”
Marty Edelston sends out balloon bouquets when you get a control. If it was publishing,
every time I showed up they had a billboard in the lobby, “Welcome, Clayton Makepeace.”
And sometimes they’d have on there, “30 million pieces mailed,” or whatever the number
was. And then they’d have a special brunch just in my honor. Those things mean a lot to
copywriters and to marketing people to know that they’re appreciated.
The fact that you’re working a distance from each other, it’s really important to do those
kinds of things and keep that relationship strong.
Remember, when you own the back end, if you’re making a commission on sales to
customers, anything that you do to increase the size of the customer base you’re going to
eventually get paid for, so you can afford to cut your fees dramatically.
Here at Response Ink for example, in the old days let’s say I’m working with a client on
a freelance basis. He wants me to write a new customer acquisition promo for him. He’s
going to have to pay me a $25,000 advance and $50/1,000. That’s just the way it is.
But if I own a piece of his back end, I’m willing to put together a front end promotion for the
guy to bring in lots more customers that I can then sell to for a fraction of that, just basically
cover my costs of doing it.
I’d be more than happy to do that because I know next month and the month after, I’m
going to have 5,000 more, 10,000 more, 20,000 more people to sell to on the back end,
and that’s where the money is, that’s where the response is, and that’s where the average
sale is.
Compensation options for new customer acquisition programs
• 	You can do it free as I just said.
• 	You can do it them on an hourly fee if it’s a small project.
• 	You can do them on a flat fee. I would never do that, but it’s not a bad way to get
started.
• 	Then there are advances against royalties, like I was just explaining.
• 	And then the pinnacle here is the fee plus royalty. On the advance plus royalty the

-- 18 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page  of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
advance is deducted prior to you get your first royalty payment. With fee, royalties begin
immediately.
We’ve actually really covered these but there are a couple of points here I want to make.
Number one is royalty possibilities could be cents for impression, which is a standard
direct-mail royalty, like $50/1,000 or whatever.
But let’s say you’re doing print ads. Most guys I know, if they’re doing a print ad they’ll just
take a percentage of the media budget. They’ll say, “Okay, well, if you spend $100,000
getting my copy out there, then I want 5% or 10% of that amount to compensate me for
writing the copy.”
And then there are these other things. They’re pretty self-explanatory, other ways to do
it. Again, for to-existing-customers percentage of sales or percentage of increase, and
we talked about metric-based bonuses. I put these in basically so that you can kind of
flip through them quickly when you’re doing your deals. I think they’re all pretty self-
explanatory.
You’ll see I have compensation options for the various types of deals that you’ll do. The
magic number is 10%. That’s the basis that I work with, and I came up with it because in
the mail my royalty is typically $50/1,000 and direct mail was costing $500/1,000. So, I
was getting 10% of the royalty. It works and that’s where I start from.
Now as I said, if they’re going to be deducting expenses from that then the number goes
up. It’s important to remember that it’s my magic number but it’s not sacred. If a guy has a
particularly huge market or a huge database, I will be willing to negotiate that down.
I gave you some revenue definitions here that will help you. I’m not an accountant and so
these are not official, but these are the kinds of definitions that I use with my clients, and
they should help you through the process of negotiating this.
I think that’s it. So let’s see, what kind of questions. Oh, there are contractual necessities
here that I’ve also given you a checklist for and I’ve pretty much covered most of those.
Jackie wants to know:
During the initial conversations, how do we record decisions that are made?
Just with a yellow pad. Both of you just sit there, talk them out, and make the notes, and
then I formalize them in a document and send them back. Once we have a final draft we’re
both happy with, it goes to the attorney.
April says:

-- 19 of 20 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 2 • Partnering for Profits
Transcript 	Page 20 of 20
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Would I stay away from companies that don’t have a marketing department?
It depends, April. I’ve worked with companies without marketing departments and had
good successes with them, but it just takes a little longer because as you begin to grow
them you are going to have to address the staff problem.
Mark Harrol wants to know:
What size businesses do you target?
Well, I’m not too concerned about the size of the business today, I’m concerned about the
size of the prospect base and I’m concerned that they have enough customers so that I
can move in quickly. That’s one of the things that we’re going to talk about next time, how
to create a $1 million royalty for yourself in the first month working with a new partner.
They need to have a sizable enough customer base that I can do that.
I hope that helps guys. Again, thank you for attending and I’ll see you next time. Good bye.

-- 20 of 20 --
